questions.question: Explain why blockchain is considered a distributed system. List the key characteristics it shares with traditional distributed systems.
keyPoints: Blockchain consists of multiple independent nodes communicating via message passing.
All nodes maintain copies of the same ledger (data replication).
No single central authority controls the system (decentralization).
Shared characteristics: fault tolerance, consensus, peer-to-peer communication.

questions.question: Compare and contrast centralized, distributed, and decentralized architectures. Explain how blockchain falls into these categories and what advantages decentralization provides.
keyPoints: Centralized: Single server/authority controls everything. Simple but has single point of failure.
Distributed: Computation spread across nodes but may have central coordinator. Example: CDN networks.
Decentralized: No single point of control, all nodes are equal peers. Example: Bitcoin network.
Blockchain is both distributed (data across many nodes) and decentralized (no central authority).
Advantages of decentralization: no single point of failure, censorship resistance, trustless operation.
Trade-offs: lower throughput, higher latency, more complex consensus.

questions.question: Describe how blockchain achieves fault tolerance in a distributed environment.
keyPoints: Full replication of the ledger across all nodes provides data redundancy.
Byzantine fault tolerance allows the network to function even with malicious nodes.
Consensus mechanisms ensure agreement despite node failures.
No single point of failure - the network continues as long as enough honest nodes participate.

questions.question: Explain the role of peer-to-peer networking in blockchain and how it differs from client-server architecture.
keyPoints: P2P: every node is equal, acting as both client and server.
Client-server: clients request services from a central server.
In blockchain P2P, any node can validate transactions, create blocks, and propagate data.
P2P eliminates dependency on any single node, improving resilience and censorship resistance.
